quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 9 Quiz 6
quiz.subtitle: Theorems for Intersecting Chords, Secants, and Tangents
quiz.subject: Geometry

questions.question: Two chords intersect inside a circle. The segments of one chord are $4$ and $6$. One segment of the other chord is $3$. What is the length of the other segment of the second chord?
questions.explanation: By the Intersecting Chords Theorem: the products of the segments of each chord are equal. So $4 \cdot 6 = 3 \cdot x$, giving $24 = 3x$ and $x = 8$. The second chord has segments $3$ and $8$.

questions.question: Two chords intersect inside a circle. One chord is divided into segments of length $x$ and $x + 4$. The other chord is divided into segments of length $3$ and $2x$. Find the value of $x$.
questions.explanation: Using the Intersecting Chords Theorem: $x(x + 4) = 3(2x)$. Expanding: $x^2 + 4x = 6x$, so $x^2 - 2x = 0$, giving $x(x - 2) = 0$. Since segment lengths must be positive, $x = 2$. Check: segments are $2$ and $6$ on one chord, $3$ and $4$ on the other. $2 \cdot 6 = 3 \cdot 4 = 12$. ✓

questions.question: Two secants are drawn from an external point $P$ to a circle. The first secant has an external segment of $4$ and an internal segment of $5$. The second secant has an external segment of $3$. What is the length of the second secant's internal segment?
questions.explanation: By the Secant-Secant Theorem: (external segment)(whole secant) is equal for both secants. First secant: external $= 4$, whole $= 4 + 5 = 9$, product $= 4 \cdot 9 = 36$. Second secant: external $= 3$, whole $= x$. So $3x = 36$, giving $x = 12$. The internal segment is $12 - 3 = 9$.

questions.question: Two secants are drawn from external point $P$. The first secant has external segment $3$ and internal segment $x + 2$. The second secant has external segment $4$ and internal segment $x - 1$. Find the value of $x$.
questions.explanation: Using the Secant-Secant Theorem: $3(3 + x + 2) = 4(4 + x - 1)$. Simplify whole secants: first whole $= x + 5$, second whole $= x + 3$. So $3(x + 5) = 4(x + 3)$, giving $3x + 15 = 4x + 12$, so $x = 3$. Check: first secant has external $3$, internal $5$, whole $8$, product $= 24$. Second: external $4$, internal $2$, whole $6$, product $= 24$. ✓

questions.question: A tangent segment from point $P$ to a circle is $8$ units long. A secant from $P$ has an external segment of $4$ and an internal segment of unknown length $x$. Find $x$.
questions.explanation: By the Secant-Tangent Theorem: $(\text{tangent})^2 = (\text{external})(\text{whole secant})$. So $8^2 = 4(4 + x)$, giving $64 = 16 + 4x$, so $4x = 48$ and $x = 12$. The whole secant is $4 + 12 = 16$. Check: $4 \cdot 16 = 64 = 8^2$. ✓

questions.question: A tangent segment has length $x$. A secant from the same external point has external segment $3$ and internal segment $x + 3$. Find the value of $x$.
questions.explanation: By the Secant-Tangent Theorem: $x^2 = 3(3 + x + 3) = 3(x + 6)$. So $x^2 = 3x + 18$, giving $x^2 - 3x - 18 = 0$. Factoring: $(x - 6)(x + 3) = 0$. Since $x > 0$, $x = 6$. Check: tangent $= 6$, secant external $= 3$, whole $= 12$, and $3 \cdot 12 = 36 = 6^2$. ✓

questions.question: Two chords intersect inside a circle. One chord has segments of length $5$ and $x + 2$. The other chord has segments of length $x$ and $7$. Find the value of $x$.
questions.explanation: Using the Intersecting Chords Theorem: $5(x + 2) = x \cdot 7$. Expanding: $5x + 10 = 7x$, giving $10 = 2x$ and $x = 5$. Check: first chord has segments $5$ and $7$, second chord has segments $5$ and $7$. Both products $= 35$. ✓

questions.question: A tangent and a secant meet at external point $P$. The tangent is $10$ units long, and the secant's external segment is $5$ units. Which equation correctly finds the secant's internal segment $x$?
questions.explanation: By the Secant-Tangent Theorem: $(\text{tangent})^2 = (\text{external})(\text{whole secant})$. The whole secant is the external segment plus the internal segment: $5 + x$. So the correct equation is $10^2 = 5(5 + x)$. This is different from the Pythagorean Theorem, which applies to right triangles, not secant-tangent relationships.

questions.question: From point $P$, secant $PA$ has external segment $6$ and internal segment $9$. Secant $PB$ has external segment $4$. What is the length of the whole secant $PB$?
questions.explanation: By the Secant-Secant Theorem: $6 \cdot (6 + 9) = 4 \cdot PB$. So $6 \cdot 15 = 4 \cdot PB$, giving $90 = 4 \cdot PB$ and $PB = 22.5$. The internal segment of $PB$ is $22.5 - 4 = 18.5$.

questions.question: Two pipes cross inside a circular tunnel, intersecting like chords. One pipe is divided into segments of $3$ m and $12$ m. The other pipe has one segment measuring $4$ m. What is the total length of the second pipe?
questions.explanation: By the Intersecting Chords Theorem: $3 \cdot 12 = 4 \cdot x$, where $x$ is the unknown segment. So $36 = 4x$, giving $x = 9$. The total length of the second pipe is $4 + 9 = 13$ m. Check: both products equal $36$. ✓

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered all segment relationship theorems: intersecting chords, secant-secant, and secant-tangent. You can set up and solve the correct product equations every time.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Remember: (1) Intersecting Chords: $a \cdot b = c \cdot d$, (2) Secant-Secant: $(\text{external}_1)(\text{whole}_1) = (\text{external}_2)(\text{whole}_2)$, (3) Secant-Tangent: $(\text{tangent})^2 = (\text{external})(\text{whole secant})$.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) identifying whether the segments are chords (both inside), secants (from external point), or tangent + secant, (2) setting up the correct product equation, (3) remembering that for secants, you use the whole secant length (external + internal), not just the internal part.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Three key theorems: (1) Intersecting Chords: products of segments are equal. (2) Secant-Secant: product of external and whole is equal for both secants. (3) Secant-Tangent: tangent squared equals product of external secant and whole secant. Always use the whole secant length, not just the internal part.
